contact.label: CONTACT
contact.title: Let's work together
contact.body: Reach out to talk about projects, opportunities, or anything you'd like.

case_study_futbol-talent-pro.context: FutbolTalent.Pro is an Argentine startup working to democratize access to professional football by connecting young players (ages 10–20) directly with scouts and clubs. The problem: talent in small cities or remote provinces stays off the radar of recruiters, who rely on closed networks and personal referrals. The platform proposes a mobile-first model where players can showcase their performance through stats, videos, and achievements, while scouts and clubs discover talent in an agile, measurable way.
case_study_futbol-talent-pro.role: I worked as UX/UI Designer pro bono for three months (August–October 2025) under a non-disclosure agreement. The team was small: myself, a Flutter developer, and the founders. My responsibility was to design the entire MVP experience — from early wireframes to 40 high-fidelity screens across three distinct user profiles (player, scout, club), build the full design system with light and dark modes, and coordinate with the developer to ensure technical feasibility during implementation.
case_study_futbol-talent-pro.process:
The main challenge was designing a freemium model that balanced democratic access with economic sustainability. The decision: players could create a free profile and apply to a limited number of open calls, while the premium plan unlocked unlimited applications and full access to training courses. Scouts had a similar structure for signing players. This generated internal friction — the founders wanted to maximize conversion to paid, but I argued that the free tier had to be functional enough to validate the product with real users before asking for money.

Another complex decision was the player explorer. The founders wanted an exhaustive filter system (position, age, region, stats, achievements, ranking) that grew infinite — almost a Google inside the app. I proposed a progressive approach: simple search by default (name, position, region) with an 'Advanced filters' button for power users. This reduced initial cognitive load and kept screens from getting overloaded.

For gamification, I designed a weekly ranking system, achievement badges (first goal uploaded, 10 videos posted, top scout of the month), and themed challenges to drive ongoing engagement. The challenge was maintaining visual clarity — every gamification element had to be recognizable without saturating the interface. I built a design system with consistent iconography (⚽ for players, 🏆 for achievements, 🔥 for rankings) that worked in both light and dark modes.
case_study_futbol-talent-pro.solution:
I delivered 40 high-fidelity screens organized into three complete user flows: player (sign-up, profile creation, applying to open calls, viewing scout feedback), scout (player exploration, advanced filtering, marking interest, managing signings), and club (publishing open calls, reviewing applicants, analyzing metrics). The design system included a color palette with light/dark support, a typographic system, reusable components (cards, buttons, inputs, badges), and spacing guidelines.

What I can't show due to the NDA: the final implemented screens and specific brand decisions. What I can share: low/mid-fidelity wireframes, flow diagrams, user personas, and the reasoning behind every information architecture decision.
case_study_futbol-talent-pro.results:
The project doesn't have user metrics yet (the app is still in development), but it does have business validation: the founders presented the full design to potential investors and received approval to move forward with development. The Flutter developer implemented the screens in parallel with my design work, which accelerated time-to-market and validated that every proposed interaction was technically viable.

Key takeaway: working under an NDA forced me to document design decisions exhaustively from the start, knowing I wouldn't be able to show the final product. That reinforced my ability to articulate the 'why' behind every decision, not just the 'what' was designed.

case_study_el-ritual-del-tono.context:
Guitarists trying to replicate the sound of their favorite artists face a real problem: the information is scattered and contradictory. You watch 10 YouTube videos about 'David Gilmour's tone' and each one tells you something different. Forums have technical discussions but are hard to navigate. Equipboard shows you what each artist uses, but doesn't tell you how to configure it for a specific song.

El Ritual del Tono solves this with a reverse search engine: you search by artist and song, and the platform gives you the complete signal chain (guitar → pedals → amplifier) needed to achieve that exact sound. It's not a generic gear store — it's a sonic discovery tool that also lets you buy the entire setup in one click.
case_study_el-ritual-del-tono.role: A personal full-stack project built end-to-end as a technical demonstration of the MERN stack (MongoDB, Express, React, Node.js). I worked solo: from data modeling in MongoDB to designing the interface in React with Next.js and Tailwind CSS. The goal was to build a complete application that solved a real problem in the guitar world — a niche I know well — while demonstrating skills in frontend, backend, and NoSQL database architecture.
case_study_el-ritual-del-tono.process:
The hardest technical decision was the 'One-Click Setup': letting a user add all the components of a sound (5–8 different gears) to the cart with a single button, without duplicating products, respecting stock, and keeping state in sync between backend and Context API. I solved it with a dedicated endpoint that receives an array of IDs, validates availability in MongoDB, and returns only the addable items. The frontend receives the response and updates the cart atomically.

Another complex decision was gear reuse across artists. David Gilmour and John Mayer use similar pedals — if I modeled each gear as unique per artist, the database would explode. The solution: a shared Gears collection where each Song has an array of ObjectIds pointing to reusable products. This drastically reduced content load and kept the data consistent.

To keep the project viable, I trimmed the initial scope: instead of 50 artists with 10 tones each, I focused on 13 iconic artists (Gilmour, Mayer, Frusciante, Cerati) with 2 tones each. That let me ship a functional MVP without getting stuck curating content forever. I also simplified checkout: instead of integrating Stripe or MercadoPago, the flow persists the order in MongoDB and simulates confirmation — enough to demonstrate the system's transactional capability.
case_study_el-ritual-del-tono.solution:
The final architecture: a Node.js backend with Express exposing a REST API, MongoDB as the database with three main collections (Artists with Songs as subdocuments, Gears as an independent collection, Orders for checkout), and a Next.js frontend with React Context API for cart management. The main flow: user lands → searches an artist → selects a song → sees the full signal chain with tone description and embedded YouTube video → can add gears individually or the full setup in one click → simulated checkout that persists the order.

The interface uses Tailwind CSS for a clean design that prioritizes content: artist pages highlight songs, song pages show the video and tone description first, and only then the gear needed. The cart is persistent across the session and calculates subtotals automatically.
case_study_el-ritual-del-tono.results:
The project is deployed on Vercel and works end-to-end: you can browse artists, explore tones, add products to the cart, and complete the simulated checkout. It has no real users yet — it's a technical demonstration — but it met its goal: validating that I can build a complete full-stack application from scratch.

Key takeaway: modeling data in MongoDB is fundamentally different from SQL. The decision to use subdocuments (Songs inside Artists) vs. separate collections has huge implications for performance and query complexity. I chose subdocuments because 90% of queries are 'give me every song by this artist,' which makes populate unnecessary and speeds up responses. If I were to redo it, I'd add authentication so users could create their own setups and share them with the community — turning the platform from personal curation into a guitarist social network.

case_study_cabify-music-match.context:
Ride-hailing apps have an invisible problem: auditory friction. Either there's an awkward silence for 15 minutes, or music plays that the passenger didn't pick and doesn't like, but they don't dare ask to change it. Asking for the aux cable is awkward. Asking the driver to switch stations, also. Most people just put on headphones and disconnect.

Cabify has a music option, but it only changes the radio station — a technology almost nobody uses anymore. The real problem isn't technical, it's social: how do you align the musical tastes of two people who just met, without adding friction to the trip request flow?
case_study_cabify-music-match.role: A UX/UI design project developed individually over three months as a product thinking exercise. My role covered UX research, information architecture, flow design, wireframing, high-fidelity visual design, and interactive prototyping in Figma. The goal was to design a feature that solved a real pain point — the discomfort of the ride — using existing technology (Spotify APIs) in a non-invasive way.
case_study_cabify-music-match.process:
The hardest design decision was balancing the musical tastes of driver and passenger so both felt satisfied, without either feeling they lost control. Three possible paths: (a) prioritize the passenger (they're paying), (b) prioritize the driver (it's their car), or (c) merge tastes intelligently. I chose (c) but with clear fallbacks.

The merge algorithm detects matches in genres and artists between both Spotify profiles and generates a temporary trip playlist. If there aren't enough matches, it offers pre-curated playlists by mood (energizing, relaxing, neutral) that statistically work for most people. And there's always a 'Silence' option for those who'd rather listen to nothing — validating that need was key, not everyone wants music.

Another challenge: onboarding. Linking Spotify can't add friction to the trip request flow. The solution: make linking optional and progressive. The first time a user opens the app after the feature launches, they see a skippable explanatory modal. If they skip, Music Match simply doesn't appear on their trips. If they link, the feature activates automatically on future trips with a simple toggle to disable it on days they don't want to use it.

I validated the design with navigation maps and user flows tested with real users (drivers and passengers). The most valuable feedback: most didn't know Cabify already had a music option (the radio station selector), confirming that the existing feature was poorly designed. I added a visible music icon on the trip's home screen so the functionality is discoverable without relying on hidden menus.
case_study_cabify-music-match.solution:
Music Match integrates as a non-invasive feature within Cabify's existing flow. Before the trip: the user links Spotify once from settings (optional). During the trip: when the driver is confirmed, the app automatically generates the merged playlist and shows it on screen with a preview of 3–4 songs that'll play. The passenger can accept it, swap it for a pre-curated playlist, or choose silence. The driver sees the same interface and can override if needed.

The functional Figma prototype (iPhone 14) includes: a skippable onboarding screen, Spotify linking flow with simulated OAuth, the main trip screen showing the generated playlist with basic playback controls, and settings where you can unlink the account or adjust genre preferences. The entire design respects Cabify's visual system (colors, typography, components) so it feels like a native extension, not a bolted-on feature.
case_study_cabify-music-match.results:
The project wasn't implemented — it's a speculative concept — but it validated my ability to detect real friction in existing products and propose technically viable solutions. The prototype is published on Behance and worked as a conversation tool in interviews: it demonstrates systems thinking (I didn't just design screens, I designed the merge algorithm and edge cases), user empathy (the silence mode emerged from research, not my assumption), and attention to implementation (using existing Spotify APIs instead of inventing impossible tech).

Key takeaway: the best features aren't the most complex — they're the ones that solve emotional problems (the discomfort of silence) with simple technical solutions (connecting two Spotify accounts). If I were to redo it, I'd design a post-trip feedback system so users could rate whether the playlist worked — that data would let the merge algorithm improve iteratively and turn Music Match into a real competitive differentiator for Cabify.

case_study_multibrand-design-system.context:
EdTech companies with multiple products face a common problem: each team designs independently, generating visual inconsistencies that confuse students and teachers. A button works differently on each platform. Colors shift. Navigation isn't predictable. That not only hurts the brand, it increases user cognitive load — critical in educational environments where clarity is fundamental.

No Country's brief was clear: design a multibrand Design System that lets multiple educational products share a common base (components, tokens, UX patterns) while each brand keeps its visual identity. The rule: 70% mandatory core, 30% customizable. Five weeks, a team of 6 distributed across Argentina, Chile, and Colombia, 100% remote.
case_study_multibrand-design-system.role: I worked as UX/UI Designer on a multicultural team of 6 during a No Country work simulation (November 2025). The team included UX Researchers, other UX/UI Designers, and we coordinated via Trello, Notion, FigJam, and Google Meet. My main responsibility: designing the component library in Figma applying the tokens defined by the team, documenting usage patterns, and contributing to the UX Writing system for accessibility. I also collaborated on defining the system architecture (tokens → foundations → components → patterns) and on building applied examples to validate the system worked across different brands.
case_study_multibrand-design-system.process:
The hardest decision was defining what elements belonged in the 70% core (immutable for every brand) vs. the 30% customizable. Each brand wanted creative freedom, but too much customization breaks educational consistency. We solved this with research: we analyzed real EdTech platforms (Coursera, edX, Udemy, Platzi), identified common patterns, and built user personas — not of students, but of the designers and developers who would use the system.

The core includes: grid, spacing, radii, shadows, component behavior, AA accessibility rules, and UX patterns (onboarding, error feedback, learning progress). What's customizable: primary/secondary/accent colors, a typeface (chosen from 3 pre-approved options: Inter, Rubik, Noto Serif), and iconography/illustrations with clear style rules.

Another complexity: coordinating 6 people across different time zones (GMT-3 to GMT-5). We implemented async dailies in Notion, design reviews twice a week on Meet, and a strict versioning system in Figma to avoid overwriting each other's work. I learned that clear documentation is more important than beautiful design — if a component isn't well documented (purpose, variants, states, Do's & Don'ts), nobody uses it correctly.

UX Writing was an unexpected challenge. We designed complete voice and tone guidelines for educational platforms: clear, human, motivating without being over the top, guiding without imposing. We created standardized microcopy for system states ('Loading...', 'Saved', 'Check the data'), empathetic error messages, and a brand adaptation system that lets you adjust tone without changing the fundamental voice.
case_study_multibrand-design-system.solution:
We delivered a complete Design System with five main components: (1) Token architecture (color, typography, spacing, radii, shadows, motion) implemented in Figma Variables with light/dark mode support. (2) UI component library (buttons, inputs, cards, navigation, modals, alerts, tables, progress bars) with every variant and state documented. (3) EdTech-specific UX patterns (onboarding, educational forms, progress feedback, gamification, error handling). (4) A governance system defining roles (Owner, Maintainer, Contributors), approval flow for new brands, and exception request process. (5) Centralized documentation in Notion with guides for non-technical teams, accessibility checklist, and a step-by-step sub-brand creation process.

We validated the system by creating two fictitious brands with opposing personalities (one formal/academic, one dynamic/innovative), applying only the customizable tokens. We confirmed that 70% of components were reused without changes and that both brands maintained visual coherence despite having different colors and typography.
case_study_multibrand-design-system.results:
The project wasn't deployed to production — it was an academic simulation — but it met its goal: demonstrating the ability to work on multidisciplinary remote teams under time pressure. We presented the complete system to No Country evaluators with a pitch deck explaining the problem, the solution, and the expected impact (reduced time-to-market, greater consistency, better student engagement).

Key takeaway: designing a Design System is 30% visual design and 70% governance and documentation. Beautiful components are useless if nobody knows when to use them or how to customize them without breaking the system. Multicultural collaboration taught me to communicate design decisions clearly and with justification — you can't just say 'I like it better this way,' you have to explain why that decision improves accessibility, consistency, or scalability. If I were to redo it, I'd prioritize fewer components but better documented — we tried to cover too much in 5 weeks and some patterns ended up without enough examples.

case_study_recuerdalo.context:
Older adults (70+) face an invisible barrier with technology: it's not that they can't use it, it's that current design assumes digital fluency they don't have. Health apps, reminders, online paperwork — everything is designed for people who grew up with smartphones. The result: frustration, abandonment, and dependence on family members for tasks they should be able to do on their own.

Recuérdalo was born as a university project (UX Research Techniques, 3 months) with a clear goal: design an app for reminders, cognitive stimulation, and family support specifically for older adults, grounded in real research with users in the target age range — not on assumptions about what they 'need'.
case_study_recuerdalo.role: I worked solo throughout the project. My responsibility covered complete research (semi-structured interviews, shadowing, open card sorting), qualitative analysis with affinity mapping, information architecture design, user personas and journey maps, wireframing, high-fidelity visual design, and functional prototyping in Figma. The focus was on rigorous UX methodology: every design decision had to be backed by evidence from research, not by intuition.
case_study_recuerdalo.process:
The hardest design decision was balancing autonomy vs. assistance. Older adults want to feel capable — the personal pride of overcoming challenges is huge — but they also need support when they get stuck. Designing too much help feels condescending. Designing too little drives abandonment.

The key insight came from Carlos (80) in an interview: 'They explain everything, I start fine, but if I make a mistake in the third step, I don't know how to go back. I end up closing everything and starting from scratch — and by then I'm done.' That revealed the problem isn't initial complexity, but sequential memory — forgetting where they were in a multi-step process.

The solution: design flows with visible reversible steps. Every action has a clear numeric indicator (Step 1 of 3), large 'Go back' buttons on every screen, and immediate visual confirmations (a big green check when they complete a task) that work as emotional micro-rewards. That turns obligations (taking pills) into small daily wins.

I ran open card sorting with three older users using 15 cards of potential features. The result: they grouped by emotional context, not by type of function. Four categories emerged: 'My Obligations and Health' (pills, appointments, shopping list), 'My People' (contacts, family photos, calls), 'To Entertain and Think' (memory games, news, radio), and 'Important Things and Help' (passwords, ID, help with the app). The 'Help' category was revealing — they associated it directly with sensitive information management, indicating they perceive those tasks as the riskiest and where they need the most support.
case_study_recuerdalo.solution:
The final Figma prototype implements the information architecture that emerged from the card sorting: four main sections accessible from a home menu with large icons and clear text labels. Every critical flow (scheduling a reminder, viewing medical appointments, adding an emergency contact) uses the reversible-steps system with numeric indicators, visible 'Back' buttons, and visual confirmations.

Accessibility decisions: minimum 18px typography (vs. the standard 14px), AA contrast across all elements, minimum 60×60px touch targets (vs. the general 44px recommendation) for less precise fingers, and a floating 'Help' button always visible that deploys contextual tutorials for the current step or lets them call an emergency contact.

The micro-reward system (big green check on task completion) emerged from prototype testing: users reported satisfaction at immediate visual confirmation, reducing the anxiety of 'did I do this right?'. That emotional detail is as important as technical functionality.
case_study_recuerdalo.results:
The project was presented at the university as a complete case study: documented research (recordings, transcripts, affinity diagrams), user personas with real quotes from interviewees, a journey map of Raquel navigating online paperwork, low/mid-fidelity wireframes, and a functional Figma prototype with every main screen.

Key takeaway: inclusive design isn't an aesthetic 'extra layer' you add at the end — it's the foundation of a good experience. By designing for Carlos, Raquel, and Laura, with their visual limitations, technological fears, and fragile sequential memory, I ended up creating a product that's clearer, more robust, and easier to use for everyone. The principles of inclusion (clarity, reversibility, visual confirmation, contextual help) improve everyone's experience, not just the target audience's.

If I were to redo it, I'd expand the concept with a 'Family Peace-of-Mind System': link the app with the user's children so they receive automatic notifications when their parents take medication or attend an appointment. That would reduce family anxiety and strengthen the older adult's support network — turning the app into a generational bridge, not just an individual tool.

about.title: About
about.claim: Design and code in one head.
about.background: My first professional role was building the Hotel Real Cafayate digital brand from scratch: positioning, strategy and communication operations for nearly three years. The hotel still runs today on the foundations I set, with an active and automated presence. I learned what makes an audience stay — and I also learned I wanted to be on the other side: building the platforms, not just using them. I'm currently finishing my degree in Multimedia Technology at Universidad Maimónides.
about.hybrid_path: At the same university I worked as Teaching Assistant in Interface Design — explaining usability and information architecture to others forced me to understand the fundamentals deeply. Professionally I've worked at Qualita Studio (Jr. Web Designer) and on FutbolTalentPro, where I designed the mobile-first MVP collaborating directly with developers.
about.hook: I don't just design interfaces. I prototype them in Figma, validate them with users, and code them in React.
about.mission: I'm looking for product teams where design and development don't need a translator. My goal: closing the gap between UX and code — clean handoffs, design decisions that are technically viable from day one.
